title: Retrieval Augmented Generation
readme:
# Retrieval Augmented Generation

Welcome to "Learn Retrieval Augmented Generation"... or "RAG" for short. Applications need search, and in the age of AI, search has become a _lot_ better, but also a bit more complex.

In this course, **you'll learn all about building [retrieval augmented generation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retrieval-augmented_generation) systems**. The best strategies, tools, and techniques for modern AI-powered search and retrieval.

## [!windows] for Windows Users

> [!warning]
> If you try to complete this course without [WSL 2](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) installed on Windows, you're gonna have a bad time.

## Boot.dev CLI

Throughout this course, you'll be using the Boot.dev CLI to run our tests (which are just CLI commands) against your local environment. [Install it now](https://github.com/bootdotdev/bootdev?tab=readme-ov-file#installation) if you don't already have it. All the instructions and troubleshooting info are on the GitHub page.

Verify the installation:

```bash
bootdev --version
```

_If you're stuck, reach out in the help forums of the [Discord](https://www.boot.dev/community)._

Once the `bootdev` command is working, log in and follow the instructions:

```bash
bootdev login
```

## Run vs. Submit

Lessons have a series of "commands" that run on your local machine, and a series of tests it will check against the results of the command. There are two ways to run these commands: `run` and `submit`:

1. `bootdev run <id>`: Runs the commands and shows the results. It's to be used for debugging, but it won't tell you whether or not you've passed the tests explicitly.
2. `bootdev run <id> -s`: Run the commands and gives you pass/fail feedback. It will also mark the lesson as complete on the website. If you get it wrong however, you'll potentially lose your sharpshooter spree, so be sure to use `run` first!

Copy the run/submit commands with the id ready-to-go from the test panel.

## Assignment

Once installed and you're logged in, copy and paste the `run` command from the right into your terminal and execute it. If it's doing what you'd expect (printing `it's rag, not ai slop`), then run the submit command.

title: What Is Search?
readme:
# What Is Search?

![what is search](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/what-is-search-1920x1080.mp4)

Imagine if you had to scroll through every single movie on Netflix to find the movie you're looking for.

**Search is a non-negotiable feature** of nearly all content-based web applications. In this course, we'll learn a bit about how it works, but more specifically we'll focus on how to use AI and LLMs and search features together for an **even better** user experience. There is so much more to a good search experience than exact keyword matches. We want to be able to:

- Find relevant results even if the search terms don't match exactly
- Match based on semantic meaning, not just keywords
- Understand user intent and context
- Provide succinct results that go beyond the raw data

To do so, we'll be learning about **RAG**, or "Retrieval Augmented Generation." And "retrieval" is the first component in RAG:

- **R:** **R**etrieve information via a search algorithm
- **A:** **A**ugment our instructions with the search results
- **G:** **G**enerate better, richer, and more accurate information using LLMs

![RAG](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/DZl22fo-1280x323.jpeg)

## What Will We Build?

We're going to build pieces of a make-believe product called "**Hoopla**" – A Netflix-like streaming service. Specifically, we'll be building the technologies that _power its search bar_.

## Assignment

Hoopla needs movie data to search through! Let's download the dataset we'll be using throughout this course.

1. [ ] Use `uv` ([installation docs](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/)) to create a new project. It will create the directory and also initialize a Git repo. (In our example code, we've named the project/directory `rag-search-engine` because it's envisioned as only one part of the Hoopla product.)
   ```sh
   uv init rag-search-engine
   cd rag-search-engine
   ```
2. [ ] Create a virtual environment at the top level of your project directory:
   ```sh
   uv venv
   ```
   > [!info]
   > Make sure the `venv` directory is in your `.gitignore` file. `uv init` usually adds it for you.
3. [ ] Activate the virtual environment:

   ```sh
   source .venv/bin/activate
   ```

   You should see `(rag-search-engine)` at the beginning of your terminal prompt; for example, mine is:

   ```text
   (rag-search-engine) wagslane@MacBook-Pro-2 rag-search-engine %
   ```

   > [!warning]
   > Always make sure that your virtual environment is _activated_ when running the code or using the Boot.dev CLI in this course.

4. [ ] Create a `data` directory in your project root.
5. [ ] Download the [movie dataset here](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/course-rag-movies.json) and save it as `data/movies.json`.
6. [ ] Add `/data/` to your `.gitignore` file to keep large data files out of version control.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Project Overview
readme:
# Project Overview

We'll be implementing Hoopla as a collection of command line scripts that perform various search operations on a local dataset of movies.

## Assignment

1. [ ] In your project's root, create a new `cli` directory. This is where we'll place all our entrypoint scripts to run our various search implementations.
2. [ ] Delete the current `main.py` file – we won't need it.
3. [ ] In the `cli` directory, create a new file called `keyword_search_cli.py` and paste in the following code:

   ```py
   import argparse

   def main() -> None:
       parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Keyword Search CLI")
       subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command", help="Available commands")

       search_parser = subparsers.add_parser("search", help="Search movies using BM25")
       search_parser.add_argument("query", type=str, help="Search query")

       args = parser.parse_args()

       match args.command:
           case "search":
               # print the search query here
               pass
           case _:
               parser.print_help()

   if __name__ == "__main__":
       main()
   ```

   This is just some boilerplate that uses the [argparse](https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html) library to parse command line arguments.
   - `uv run cli/keyword_search_cli.py search "your search query"` will execute the "search" case and populate "your search query" into the `args.query` variable.
   - `uv run cli/keyword_search_cli.py` with no (or invalid) arguments will print the parser's help message.

4. [ ] Update the "search" case to print the search query in this format:

   ```text
   Searching for: QUERY
   ```

   _Where `QUERY` is the value of `args.query`._

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

CLICommand.Command: uv run cli/keyword_search_cli.py search "boots the bear"

title: Keyword Search
readme:
# Keyword Search

Before we get deep into the advanced techniques of RAG, let's start with more "basic" search. That way we can understand the benefits of the features we'll be building on top of. _Keyword search_ is exactly what it sounds like: we check if the user-supplied search terms exist in the content in our dataset, and return any results that match.

Almost every (even RAG) search implementation uses keyword search as a foundational component. It's fast, simple, and effective for many use cases.

The more technical and precise the terminology in the domain is, the more powerful keyword search is! For example:

**Medical Search – Precision Desired**

- **Query:** "COVID-19"
- **Keyword search:** ✅ Finds documents specifically about COVID-19
- **Semantic-only search:** ❌ Finds less-related content about "respiratory viruses" or "pandemic diseases"

**Movie Search – Generalization Desired**

- **Query:** "Alien movie"
- **Keyword search:** ❗Only finds "Alien"
- **Semantic-only search:** ✅ Finds similar movies like "Invaders from Mars", "E.T.", or "Alien"

## Assignment

Let's make our "search" command _actually work_!

> [!isaac]
> I'm not going to be overly prescriptive about how you structure your code as far as file/directory structure goes. I'm going to describe the expected behavior. If you'd like to follow my structure, simply check the solutions after each step.

1. [ ] Load the `data/movies.json` file into a Python dictionary using the [`json.load`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.load) function.
2. [ ] Iterate over all the movies in the list stored under the `movies` key, and check their "title" field. If the title contains the search query, append the movie to a results list.
3. [ ] Print the results in this format:
   ```text
   Searching for: QUERY
   1. Movie Title 1
   2. Movie Title 2
   3. Movie Title 3
   ...
   ```
4. [ ] Truncate the list to a maximum of 5 results ordered by ascending ID (which the dataset already is).

> [!warning]
> Notice that this search is case sensitive and only matches exact substrings. Don't worry, we'll improve it soon enough!

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Text Processing
readme:
# Text Processing

**Exact** match, frankly, is not particularly useful. It's quite rare that a user searching for "The Alien" wants us to exclude results like:

- "the alien" (different case)
- "Alien" (missing "The")
- "Aliens" (plural form)

Keyword search doesn't need to be exact, in fact, it _shouldn't_ be. To improve keyword search we're going to need to do some **text processing**. For example, we want `bear` and `Bear` and `bear.` to all be identified as the _same keyword_. So, we're going to do a few things.

## Text Processing Pipeline

![Text Processing](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/QpkclET-620x720.png)

- **Case insensitivity:** Convert all text to lowercase
  - `"The Matrix"` becomes `"the matrix"`
  - `"HE IS HERE"` becomes `"he is here"`
- **Remove punctuation:** We don't care about periods, commas, etc
  - `"Hello, world!"` becomes `"hello world"`
  - `"sci-fi"` becomes `"scifi"`
- **Tokenization:** Break text into individual words
  - `"the matrix"` becomes `["the", "matrix"]`
  - `"hello world"` becomes `["hello", "world"]`
- **Stop words:** Remove common [stop words](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stop_word) that don't add much meaning
  - `["the", "matrix"]` becomes `["matrix"]`
  - `["a", "puppy"]` becomes `["puppy"]`
- **Stemming:** Keep only the [stem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem) of words
  - `["running", "jumping"]` becomes `["run", "jump"]`
  - `["watching", "windmills"]` becomes `["watch", "windmill"]`

## Assignment

Hoopla users can't be bothered to use the _shift_ key!

**Update your matching logic** to [`.lower()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.lower) case both the search query and the movie titles before comparing them.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Punctuation
readme:
# Punctuation

We're going to keep it _really_ simple for now by removing all punctuation from the text in our dataset.

**Examples:**

- `Boots the bear!` -> `Boots the bear`
- `The wonderful bear, Boots` -> `The wonderful bear Boots`

There are cases in which this doesn't work (e.g., hyphens) but we don't have to worry about them for our purposes.

## Assignment

**Remove all punctuation** from both the query and the title before attempting to match them.

Here are some of the standard library functions I used:

- [`.translate()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.translate)
- [`str.maketrans()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.maketrans)
- [`string.punctuation`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#string.punctuation)

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Tokenization
readme:
# Tokenization

[**Tokenization**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Search_engine_indexing#Tokenization) means splitting text into smaller pieces, called **tokens**. Simple **word-based tokenization** looks like this:

- **Input:** `"The Matrix is a great movie!"`
- **Output:** `["the", "matrix", "is", "a", "great", "movie"]`

Many tokenization methods exist, but we'll use word-based tokenization for keyword search. We already handled case insensitivity and punctuation removal in the previous steps.

> [!info]
> You might hear ['tokens' in the context of LLMs](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/4936856-what-are-tokens-and-how-to-count-them) don't map one-to-one with words, and that's true, so don't let that trip you up here.

## Assignment

We want Hoopla users to be able to search for _partial matches_, not just exact substrings.

For example, the query "Great Bear" will match the title "Big Bear" because the "bear" token is present in both.

> [!tip]
> Wrap this logic in a helper function! You'll need to apply the exact same processing throughout this course.

1. [ ] Split the query and the title into tokens
   - [`.split()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.split) on whitespace
   - Remove any empty tokens
2. [ ] Update your matching logic to allow matches where at least one token from the query matches _any part_ of a token from the title. (Stemming not required.)

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Stop Words
readme:
# Stop Words

Now that we have a list of tokens, we need to decide which ones are _actually useful_ for search. Not all tokens are created equal...

## Examples of High-Value Tokens

- bear
- panda
- london
- protector

## Examples of Low-Value Tokens

- a
- the
- is
- of
- in

These are examples of [**stop words**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stop_word), which is a term for "words that don't have much semantic meaning". In short, _we're going to remove them_.

## Why Remove Stop Words?

Our goal is to match words from the user's input to the words in our dataset. But if we don't remove stop words, we can get some pretty misleading results, here's an example:

**User query:** "the bear"

**Results:**

- "Jungle Bear": matches "bear".
- "The Terminator": matches "the".

**Oof!** Both documents match, but "Jungle Bear" is clearly more relevant! "the Terminator" only matches on a meaningless stop word.

## Assignment

**Remove stop words tokens** from both the user query and the title tokens.

1. [ ] Create a new `data/stopwords.txt` file and paste the words at the end of this page into it.
2. [ ] Read the stop words from the file and store them in a list.
   - [`.read()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.TextIOBase.read) the file
   - [`.splitlines()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.splitlines) to split it into a list of words
3. [ ] Remove any stop words from the user query tokens and the title tokens before your matching logic runs.

title: Stemming
readme:
# Stemming

Now that we have a list of tokens, we need to reduce them to their _root form_. This helps match different variations of the same word.

## Examples of Word Variations

**Stemming Examples:**

- running, runs, ran → **run**
- jumping, jumps, jumped → **jump**
- watching, watches, watched → **watch**

![Stemming](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/X2Xj8c4-777x682.png)

These variations are reduced to their [stem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stemming), which is the base form of a word. This process is called stemming.

## Why Stem Words?

Our goal is to match words from the user's input to the words in our dataset. But if we don't stem words, we can fail to retrieve some valid results. Here's an example:

User query: **"running"**

- **A River Runs Through It:** Not returned – no exact match for "running"
- **Run Baby Run:** Not returned – no exact match for "running"

**Ugh!** Both contain the concept, but in different forms that don't quite match the query.

## Assignment

Implementing stemming from scratch is a lot of work, so we'll use the [`nltk.stem`](https://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.stem.html) library to handle it for us.

1. [ ] Install the `nltk` library:
   ```sh
   uv add nltk==3.9.1
   ```
2. [ ] Import the [`PorterStemmer`](https://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.stem.porter.html#nltk.stem.porter.PorterStemmer) class from `nltk.stem`:
   ```py
   from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer
   ```
3. [ ] Create an instance of `PorterStemmer`:
   ```py
   stemmer = PorterStemmer()
   ```
4. [ ] After removing stop words from the tokens, reduce each token to its root (stemmed form) before matching. Use [`stemmer.stem(token)`](https://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.stem.porter.html#nltk.stem.porter.PorterStemmer.stem) to get the stemmed form of each token.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Inverted Index
readme:
# Inverted Index

An [inverted index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverted_index) is what makes search fast – it's like a [SQL database index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Database_index), but for text search. Instead of scanning every document each time a user searches, we build an index for fast lookups.

> [!info]
> A "forward index" maps location -> value. An "**inverted** index" maps value -> location.

Here's an **example** mapping words to the document IDs they appear in (`word -> document IDs`):

- `matrix` -> `[1, 5, 10]`
- `hacker` -> `[1, 8]`
- `reality` -> `[1, 3, 7]`

> [!isaac]
> _Using_ an inverted index is super fast – we get `O(1)` lookups on each token. However, _building_ the index is slow, because we have to read every document and tokenize it.

## Assignment

**Implement an `InvertedIndex` class** and use it for keyword search.

1. [ ] Create a new `InvertedIndex` class. It should have:
   1. [ ] An `index` attribute – a dictionary mapping tokens (strings) to _sets_ of document IDs (integers).
   2. [ ] A `docmap` attribute – a dictionary mapping document IDs to their full document objects.
   3. [ ] An `__add_document(self, doc_id, text)` method. Tokenize the input text, then add each token to the index with the document ID.
   4. [ ] A `get_documents(self, term)` method. It should get the set of document IDs for a given token, and return them as a _list_, [sorted](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#sorted) in ascending order. For our purposes, you can assume that the input term is a single word/token – though you may still want to lowercase it for good measure.
   5. [ ] A `build` method. It should iterate over all the movies and add them to both the index and the docmap.
      - When adding the movie data to the index with `__add_document()`, concatenate the title and the description and use that as the input text. For example:
        - `f"{m['title']} {m['description']}"`
   6. [ ] A `save` method. It should save the index and docmap attributes to disk using the [`pickle`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html) module's [`dump`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#pickle.dump) function.
      1. [ ] Use the file path/name `cache/index.pkl` for the index.
      2. [ ] Use the file path/name `cache/docmap.pkl` for the docmap.
      3. [ ] Have this method create the `cache` directory if it doesn't exist (before trying to write files into it).
2. [ ] Add a new "build" command to the `keyword_search_cli.py` entrypoint script.
   1. [ ] It should build the inverted index and save it to disk.
   2. [ ] After doing so, it should print a message containing the first ID of the document for the token `'merida'` (which should be document 4651, `"Brave"`).

```py
print(f"First document for token 'merida' = {docs[0]}")
```

> [!warning]
> We'll hardcode this as a very simple test to ensure that the index is built correctly.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Use the Index
readme:
# Use the Index

Now that we've built the index and saved it to disk, we can _use_ it to quickly search for documents. Assuming our database doesn't change, we don't need to rebuild the index every time we search.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Delete the hardcoded "merida" search test from your build command.
2. [ ] Add a `load()` method to your `InvertedIndex` class. It should load the index and docmap from disk using the [`pickle`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html) module's [`load`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#pickle.load) function.
   - Use `cache/index.pkl` for the index
   - Use `cache/docmap.pkl` for the docmap
   - It should raise an error if the files don't exist.
3. [ ] Update your search command to load the index from disk. If it doesn't exist, just print an error message and exit.
4. [ ] Remove your logic for iterating over queries and movies.
5. [ ] Instead, iterate over each token in the query, and **use the inverted index** to get any matching documents for each token. Once you have 5 results, stop searching and just return them. _Print the resulting document titles and IDs_.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Boolean Search
readme:
# Boolean Search

Let's make our keyword search more powerful by adding support for boolean operations like `AND`, `OR`, and `NOT`. For example:

- "bear AND wizard"
- "bear NOT terror"
- "bear OR cyborg"

Remember, thanks to our inverted index, each of these words maps to a set of document IDs:

- `bear` -> `[1, 3, 5, 7, 9]`
- `wizard` -> `[2, 4, 6, 8]`
- `terror` -> `[7, 9]`
- `cyborg` -> `[2, 6]`

So each boolean operation is really just a set operation on those document IDs:

- **AND** = Set [intersection](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intersection_(set_theory)>) (∩)
- **OR** = Set [union](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Union_(set_theory)>) (∪)
- **NOT** = Set [complement](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complement_(set_theory)>) (−)

And it will be incredibly _fast_ because we're working with sets of numbers, not massive text documents.

## Boolean Operations in Detail

### AND Operation (Intersection)

"bear AND forest" finds documents that contain **both** terms:

```text
bear:    [1] [3] [5] [7] [9]
forest:  [1] [4] [5] [8]
AND:     [1]     [5]        # Only overlapping documents
```

### OR Operation (Union)

```text
bear:   [1] [3] [5] [7] [9]
cyborg:     [2]         [6]
OR:     [1] [2] [3] [5] [6] [7] [9]  # All documents combined
```

### NOT Operation (Difference)

"bear NOT terror" finds documents with "bear" but excluding those with "terror":

```text
bear:   [1] [3] [5] [7] [9]
terror:             [7] [9]
NOT:    [1] [3] [5]         # Bear docs minus terror docs
```

> [!isaac]
> We're not going to _implement_ boolean operations because it's a lot of code that we would quickly overwrite anyhow with some more advanced techniques in the next chapter.

title: Term Frequency
readme:
# Term Frequency

Now that we can find the documents that contain our search terms, let's consider how _often_ terms appear in documents.

> Early internet search engines (pre-Google) heavily used term frequency to rank results... sites could game rankings by repeating keywords over and over (keyword stuffing). Luckily, Google and others now rely on more advanced techniques.

[Term frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tf%E2%80%93idf#:~:text=Term%20frequency%5Bedit%5D) (TF) measures how often a word appears in a document. For example:

> Boots the bear teaches **students** programming while enjoying his _salmon_ for lunch. The **students** gather around eagerly as they learn debugging techniques, and all **students** leave with new coding skills.

**Term Frequencies:**

- `students` appears 3 times
- `salmon` appears 1 time

This is a signal that `student` is likely more important than `salmon` (which it is, even if the gluttonous bear happens to not agree).

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a new `term_frequencies` attribute to your `InvertedIndex` class. It's a dictionary of document IDs to [`Counter`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.Counter) objects. A `Counter` is a dictionary optimized for counting. We'll be keeping track of how many times each term appears in each document.
2. [ ] Update your `load` and `save` methods to also load and save this new attribute into a new `cache/term_frequencies.pkl` file.
3. [ ] Update your `__add_document` method to update the term frequencies for each token in the document. For each token, increment its count in the `Counter` for that document ID.
4. [ ] Add a new `get_tf(self, doc_id, term)` method. It should return the times the token appears in the document with the given ID. If the term doesn't exist in that document, return 0. Be sure to tokenize the term, but assume that there is only one token. If there's more than one, raise an exception.
5. [ ] Add a new "tf" command to the `keyword_search_cli.py` entrypoint script.
   1. [ ] It should take a document ID and a term as arguments.

title: Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)
readme:
# Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

[Inverse Document Frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tf%E2%80%93idf#:~:text=%7D-,Inverse%20document%20frequency,-%5Bedit%5D) handles common words that are _specific to our dataset_ (as opposed to generic stopwords). Consider our dataset about bear movies.

> "A movie about an actor who becomes a coding instructor"

`actor` may be a great keyword to narrow the search in _some_ datasets... but many movies talk about actors!

Document frequency (DF) measures how many documents in the dataset contain a term. The more documents a term appears in, the bigger its value. And that answers the next big question: "**why inverse?**" It's because we want _rare_ terms to have _higher_ scores. Say we have `100` documents in total:

Common term (appears in 95 documents):

- `bear`: IDF = log(100/95) = 0.02 ← Low score

Rare term (appears in 2 docs):

- `cyborg`: IDF = log(100/2) = 1.7 ← High score

Universal term (appears in all docs):

- `movie`: IDF = log(100/100) = 0 ← Zero score

## The Formula

```py
math.log((total_doc_count + 1) / (term_match_doc_count + 1))
```

> [!warning]
> The `+1` prevents division by zero errors when a term doesn't appear in any documents.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add an `idf` command to your `keyword_search_cli.py` entrypoint script:
   1. [ ] Take a term as an argument.
   2. [ ] Load the index and docmap from disk using the `load()` method you implemented in the previous assignment.
   3. [ ] Calculate the IDF for the given term using the formula above.
   4. [ ] Print the IDF value.
2. [ ] Format the floating point value to 2 decimal places when printing:

```py
print(f"Inverse document frequency of '{args.term}': {idf:.2f}")
```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

> [!info]
> Your IDF results depend on the exact contents of `movies.json` and `stopwords.txt`. If your values are close but not identical to the tests, make sure you haven't edited these files.

title: TF-IDF
readme:
# TF-IDF

![TF-IDF](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/tf-idf-1920x1080.mp4)

Term Frequency and Inverse Document Frequency are often used together to create their ultimate form: [TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tf%E2%80%93idf). TF-IDF combines both metrics:

**TF-IDF Components:**

1. **TF (Term Frequency):** How often a term appears in a document
2. **IDF (Inverse Document Frequency):** How rare a term is across all documents

The formula is simple:

```text
TF-IDF = TF * IDF
```

- **Frequent** words get high TF scores
- **Rare** words get high IDF scores
- **Best matches** have both high TF _and_ high IDF

## Example

Suppose we search a dataset of 3 movies for "cyborg bear".

**Document 1:** "The Terminator – A cyborg from the future"

- "cyborg": TF=1, IDF=3.9 → TF-IDF = 1 × 3.9 = 3.9
- "bear": TF=0, IDF=0.05 → TF-IDF = 0 × 0.05 = 0
- Total score: 3.9

**Document 2:** "Ted – A talking bear who loves honey and bear friends"

- "cyborg": TF=0, IDF=3.9 → TF-IDF = 0 × 3.9 = 0
- "bear": TF=2, IDF=0.05 → TF-IDF = 2 × 0.05 = 0.1
- Total score: 0.1

**Document 3:** "Cyborg Bear – A robotic bear saves the city"

- "cyborg": TF=1, IDF=3.9 → TF-IDF = 1 × 3.9 = 3.9
- "bear": TF=2, IDF=0.05 → TF-IDF = 2 × 0.05 = 0.1
- Total score: 4.0

**Final Rankings:**

1. Document 3 (Cyborg Bear) – 4.0 – Has both terms!
2. Document 1 (Terminator) – 3.9 – Has rare "cyborg" term
3. Document 2 (Ted) – 0.1 – Only common "bear" term

This makes perfect sense! The movie that's actually about a cyborg bear ranks highest, and "cyborg" is more important than "bear" in the context of our bear-only dataset.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a `tfidf` command to your `keyword_search_cli.py` entrypoint script:
   1. [ ] Take a document ID and term as arguments.
   2. [ ] Load the index and docmap from disk using the `load()` method you implemented in the previous assignment.
   3. [ ] Calculate the TF-IDF score for the given document and term using the formula above.
   4. [ ] Print the TF-IDF value.
2. [ ] Format the floating point value to 2 decimal places when printing:
   ```py
   print(f"TF-IDF score of '{args.term}' in document '{args.doc_id}': {tf_idf:.2f}")
   ```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: BM25
readme:
# BM25

While TF-IDF is a good start, there's a better algorithm called [Okapi BM25](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Okapi_BM25) that improves upon TF-IDF.

## What Makes BM25 Better?

BM25 addresses three key problems with basic TF-IDF:

1. **Better IDF calculation:** More stable scoring for rare/common terms
2. **Term frequency saturation:** Prevents terms from dominating by appearing too often
3. **Document length normalization:** Accounts for longer vs shorter documents

## The IDF Problem

In basic TF-IDF, **IDF** is calculated as `log(N / df)`:

- **N** = total number of documents in the collection
- **df** = document frequency (how many documents contain this term)
- **log** = logarithm function (reduces the impact of very large numbers)

The problems with this formula are:

- **Division by zero:** When `df = 0` (we "solved" this by adding 1 to both the numerator and denominator)
- **Unstable rare terms:** Very rare terms get extremely high scores
- **Negative scores:** Very common terms can get negative scores

## BM25 IDF Solution

BM25 uses a more _stable_ IDF formula:

```py
IDF = log((N - df + 0.5) / (df + 0.5) + 1)
```

Let's break down the reasoning behind each piece, starting with the core ratio:

```py
(N - df + 0.5) / (df + 0.5)
```

This compares the documents that **don't contain** the term against documents that **do contain** it:

**Components breakdown:**

- **Numerator `(N - df + 0.5)`:** Count of documents WITHOUT the term (plus smoothing)
- **Denominator `(df + 0.5)`:** Count of documents WITH the term (plus smoothing)

### Smoothing?

- **Why Add 0.5?** To prevent division by `0`. The fancy term for this is [Laplace smoothing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Additive_smoothing).
- **Why Add 1?** The final `+ 1` ensures IDF is always positive, which, again, just handles some edge cases.

## Assignment

In this lesson we'll focus on the first improvement: **better IDF calculation**.

1. [ ] Add a new method, with a signature like `get_bm25_idf(self, term: str) -> float`, to your `InvertedIndex` class.
   1. [ ] Use the BM25 IDF formula: `log((N - df + 0.5) / (df + 0.5) + 1)`, where `N` is the total number of documents and `df` is the document frequency.
   2. [ ] Tokenize the term and validate that it's a single token (raise an exception if not).
   3. [ ] Return the calculated BM25 IDF score.
2. [ ] Add a function that allows this new method to be called and tested. I named mine `bm25_idf_command`. This function should:
   1. [ ] Accept a single argument – the term to get the BM25 IDF score for.
   2. [ ] Load the inverted index from disk.
   3. [ ] Call the `get_bm25_idf` method (or whatever you named it).
   4. [ ] Return the BM25 IDF score for the term, as a float.
3. [ ] Back in your entrypoint script, i.e. `keyword_search_cli.py`, add a new `bm25idf` command:
   ```py
   bm25_idf_parser = subparsers.add_parser("bm25idf", help="Get BM25 IDF score for a given term")
   bm25_idf_parser.add_argument("term", type=str, help="Term to get BM25 IDF score for")
   ```
4. [ ] Handle the `bm25idf` command in your match statement:
   1. [ ] Call the BM25 IDF command function.
   2. [ ] Print the result formatted to 2 decimal places (using whatever variable name you assigned):
      ```py
      print(f"BM25 IDF score of '{args.term}': {bm25idf:.2f}")
      ```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Term Frequency Saturation
readme:
# Term Frequency Saturation

Let's tackle the second BM25 improvement: **term frequency saturation**. This prevents any single term from dominating search results just because it appears _many many_ times.

## The Term Frequency Problem

In basic TF-IDF, if a word appears `100` times, it gets **10x** more weight than a word that appears `10` times. This creates problems:

**Query: "bear hunting"**

- Document A: "bear bear bear bear" → 4 matches
- Document B: "bear hunting guide for beginners" → 2 matches

With basic TF, Document A gets a much higher score despite being clearly less useful!

## The Saturation Solution

BM25 uses **diminishing returns** – after a certain point, additional occurrences matter less. Let's break down the formula:

```py
tf_component = (tf * (k1 + 1)) / (tf + k1)
```

`k1` is a tunable parameter that controls the diminishing returns (a common value is `1.5`). Let's see how different TF values get "saturated":

| Term Frequency | Basic TF | BM25 TF (k1=1.5) |
| -------------- | -------- | ---------------- |
| 1              | 1        | 1.0              |
| 2              | 2        | 1.4              |
| 5              | 5        | 1.9              |
| 10             | 10       | 2.2              |
| 20             | 20       | 2.3              |

Notice how BM25 grows much slower – the first few occurrences matter the most!

![Basic vs BM25 TF Growth](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/NirA8rq-552x447.png)

## Assignment

Implement the BM25 term frequency saturation formula in our `InvertedIndex` class. Use a `k1` value of `1.5` to control the saturation effect.

1. [ ] Define a constant to use as a default value for `k1`. (You might find it useful to place constants like this in a dedicated module, so they can be reused across different parts of your codebase.)
   ```py
   BM25_K1 = 1.5
   ```
2. [ ] Add a new method `get_bm25_tf(self, doc_id, term, k1=BM25_K1)` to your `InvertedIndex` class:
   1. [ ] Get the raw term frequency using your existing `get_tf` method
   2. [ ] Apply the BM25 saturation formula: `(tf * (k1 + 1)) / (tf + k1)`
   3. [ ] Return the saturated TF score
3. [ ] Add a new `bm25_tf_command` function that:
   1. [ ] Takes `doc_id`, `term`, and optional `k1` parameters
   2. [ ] Loads the index from disk
   3. [ ] Calls the `get_bm25_tf` method with the provided parameters
   4. [ ] Returns the BM25 TF score
4. [ ] Add a new `bm25tf` command to the `keyword_search_cli.py` entry point script:
   ```py
   bm25_tf_parser = subparsers.add_parser(
     "bm25tf", help="Get BM25 TF score for a given document ID and term"
   )
   bm25_tf_parser.add_argument("doc_id", type=int, help="Document ID")
   bm25_tf_parser.add_argument("term", type=str, help="Term to get BM25 TF score for")
   bm25_tf_parser.add_argument("k1", type=float, nargs='?', default=BM25_K1, help="Tunable BM25 K1 parameter")
   ```
5. [ ] Handle the command in your match statement:
   1. [ ] Call the `bm25_tf_command` function with the provided arguments.
   2. [ ] Print the result, formatted to 2 decimal places:
      ```py
      print(f"BM25 TF score of '{args.term}' in document '{args.doc_id}': {bm25tf:.2f}")
      ```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Document Length Normalization
readme:
# Document Length Normalization

The final BM25 improvement is **document length normalization** – ensuring longer documents don't get unfair advantages over shorter, more focused ones. Longer documents contain more words, which can artificially boost their scores:

**Query:** "bear"

- Document A: "Boots is a silly bear wizard"
- Document B: "Ted is a wonderful, amazing, fantastic human who has a stuffed bear that loves honey, salmon, picnics, and hanging out with other bears in the woods. Ted's bear is so nice to hang out with Ted all day long."

Document B has higher term frequencies because it's longer, but **not** because it's more relevant!

## The Length Normalization Solution

BM25 adjusts term frequency based on document length:

```py
# Length normalization factor
length_norm = 1 - b + b * (doc_length / avg_doc_length)

# Apply to term frequency
tf_component = (tf * (k1 + 1)) / (tf + k1 * length_norm)
```

Let's break it down.

### The Core Ratio: `doc_length / avg_doc_length`

This ratio tells us how this document's length compares to the average document length in the dataset:

| Ratio | Meaning              | Effect    |
| ----- | -------------------- | --------- |
| = 1.0 | Average length       | No change |
| > 1.0 | Longer than average  | Penalized |
| < 1.0 | Shorter than average | Boosted   |

### **b (Normalization Strength)**

`b` is a tunable parameter that controls how much we care about document length.

- If `b=0` then length norm is always `1`.
- If `b=1` then full normalization is applied.

The key insight is:

- **Long documents** get higher `length_norm` and are **penalized** (lower scores)
- **Short documents** get lower `length_norm` and are **boosted** (higher scores)

> [!tip]
> A common value is `0.75`, which tends to work well in most scenarios.

## Assignment

Implement the BM25 document length normalization formula in our `InvertedIndex` class. Use a `b` value of `0.75` and a `k1` value of `1.5` to control the saturation effect.

1. [ ] Add a new constant for the `b` parameter at the top of your module `search_utils.py`:
   ```py
   BM25_B = 0.75
   ```
2. [ ] Add a new `doc_lengths` attribute to your `InvertedIndex` class:
   1. [ ] Initialize it as an empty dictionary in `__init__`
   2. [ ] Create a new cache file path: `self.doc_lengths_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, "doc_lengths.pkl")`
3. [ ] Update your `__add_document` method to track document lengths:
   1. [ ] Count the total number of tokens in each document (after tokenization)
   2. [ ] Store this count in the `self.doc_lengths` dictionary by `doc_id`
4. [ ] Update your `save` and `load` methods:
   1. [ ] Save the `doc_lengths` dictionary to the cache file
   2. [ ] Load the `doc_lengths` dictionary from the cache file
5. [ ] Add a private helper method `__get_avg_doc_length(self) -> float`:
   1. [ ] Calculate and return the average document length across all documents
   2. [ ] Handle the edge case where there are no documents (return 0.0)
6. [ ] Update your `get_bm25_tf` method to include document length normalization:
   1. [ ] Add a `b` parameter with default value `BM25_B`
   2. [ ] Calculate the length normalization
   3. [ ] Update the final formula to use length normalization
7. [ ] Update the `bm25_tf_command` function and CLI parser:
   1. [ ] Add support for an optional `b` parameter
   2. [ ] Update the parser to accept both `k1` and `b` arguments:
      ```py
      bm25_tf_parser.add_argument("b", type=float, nargs='?', default=BM25_B, help="Tunable BM25 b parameter")
      ```
8. [ ] Rerun `uv run cli/keyword_search_cli.py build` to store the document lengths.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: BM25 Search
readme:
# BM25 Search

In the last chapter we implemented TF-IDF, and in this chapter we've made key improvements needed for BM25 search:

- More stable IDF calculation
- Term Frequency saturation handling
- Document length Normalization

We're ready to put it all together to implement BM25.

## BM25 Search Algorithm

The BM25 formula multiplies TF by IDF (like TF-IDF), but uses the improved versions we've built in the last few lessons:

```py
BM25 = bm25_tf * bm25_idf
```

This gives us a score for a `document` and `term`. To calculate the full BM25 score we need to aggregate it up.

1. Tokenize our query.
2. Calculate the BM25 score for each token in the query for the document, and sum them.
3. Do this for every document.
4. Sort documents by score (highest first).
5. Return the top N documents.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a new `bm25(self, doc_id, term)` method to the`InvertedIndex`class in`lib/keyword_search.py`:
   1. [ ] Use your `get_bm25_tf` and `get_bm25_idf` methods to get the score components.
   2. [ ] Return the full BM25 score, i.e., `bm25_tf * bm25_idf`.
2. [ ] Add a new `bm25_search(self, query, limit)` method to the `InvertedIndex` class:
   1. [ ] Tokenize the query.
   2. [ ] Initialize a scores dictionary, to map document IDs to their total BM25 scores.
   3. [ ] For each document in the index, calculate the total BM25 score:
      1. [ ] For each query token, add its BM25 score to the document's running total.
      2. [ ] Store the total score in the scores dictionary.
   4. [ ] Sort the documents by score in descending order.
   5. [ ] Return the top `limit` documents along with their scores.
3. [ ] Add a `bm25search` command to your CLI in `keyword_search_cli.py`. It should accept, at minimum, a query as a positional argument. You may also like to add an optional `--limit` argument (defaulting to 5).
   ```py
   bm25search_parser = subparsers.add_parser("bm25search", help="Search movies using full BM25 scoring")
   bm25search_parser.add_argument("query", type=str, help="Search query")
   ```
4. [ ] The command should use the `InvertedIndex` to load the movie data and perform the BM25 search.
5. [ ] Print the results in this format:

   ```text
   1. (15) The Adventures of Mowgli - Score: 7.79
   2. (11342) Gakuen Alice - Score: 7.42
   3. (30) Day of the Animals - Score: 7.21
   4. (5542) Candy - Score: 7.07
   5. (3395) Life of Pi - Score: 7.05
   ```

   _The number in the parentheses is the document ID._

title: Semantic Search
readme:
# Semantic Search

![Semantic Search](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/semantic-search-1920x1080.mp4)

Keyword and metadata search are great, but they have some serious limitations. Have you ever searched "baggins" on Netflix, and it returns "The Hobbit"? Keyword search _can't do that_. It can only match when a keyword appears in the target document.

[Semantic search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semantic_search), on the other hand, can find results where the words have similar _meaning_, rather than similar _spelling_. It finds relevant documents even when they don't contain your exact search terms!

**Bear Movie Search Example:**

Query: "exciting adventure"

- **Keyword Search**
  - Searches for: "exciting" AND "adventure"
  - Misses: Films described as "thrilling journey"
- **Semantic Search**
  - Understands: You want action-packed films
  - Finds: Movies with "gripping", "suspenseful", "journey"

## When Semantic Search Is Better

- **Synonym matching:** "Happy movies" → finds "joyful" or "uplifting" films
- **Conceptual queries:** "Movies about friendship" → finds films exploring character bonds
- **Natural language:** "What bear movies are good for kids?" → understands family-friendly content

## When Keyword Search Is Better

- **Exact terms:** Medical terms like "COVID-19"
- **Proper nouns:** Specific titles like "The Matrix"
- **Technical jargon:** Programming terms like "dependency injection"

## Assignment

Let's build semantic search! We'll use a _new_ CLI script specifically for semantic search operations. I'll give you the boilerplate.

In the `cli` directory, create a new file called `semantic_search_cli.py` and paste in the following code:

```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import argparse

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Semantic Search CLI")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    match args.command:
        case _:
            parser.print_help()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Embeddings
readme:
# Embeddings

The fundamental tool that will power our semantic search is [**embeddings**](https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/ai/what-are-embeddings/): numerical representations of text that capture the _meaning_ of words.

Specifically, our embeddings are [vectors](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_(mathematics_and_physics)>): a fancy word for a **list of numbers**. A vector is a list of numbers that represents a point in space, specifically, a _direction_ and a _magnitude_ from the origin `(0, 0)`.

![vector despicable me meme](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/2lKPehm-715x720.png)

Here are two 2D vectors:

- **Red:** `[3, 2]` (3 units right, 2 units up)
- **Blue:** `[1, 2]` (1 unit right, 2 units up)

![vectors](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/Tk9g3Ni-1075x720.png)

The "embedding" happens when we take a piece of text and convert it into a vector. For example:

- "King" -> `[3.5, 2.5]`
- "Queen" -> `[3.0, 2.0]`
- "Human" -> `[3.0, -3.0]`

![vector embeddings](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/XBlLQ2f-795x720.png)

The idea is that the **distance** between the vectors represents how similar the meanings of the words are. King and queen are very similar, and therefore their vectors are close together. Human is less similar, so its vector is further away.

> [!info]
> Numbers in vectors are sometimes normalized to a smaller scale of floating point numbers like `-1.0` to `1.0` for easier comparison.

question.question: What's the main reason that we represent text as vectors (embeddings) in semantic search?
answers: To reduce text size so it takes up less memory
To ensure that every word has a unique coordinate in 2D space
To convert text into binary for faster storage and retrieval
To capture the meaning of text, with distances between vectors reflecting similarity
question.correctAnswer: To capture the meaning of text, with distances between vectors reflecting similarity

title: Embedding Models
readme:
# Embedding Models

The process of converting text into vectors requires a lot of data and computation – it's a machine learning "training" process that basically slurps up a _massive_ amount of text data to learn patterns about how different words and phrases relate to each other.

For our purposes, there isn't much to be gained by training our own model from scratch, so we'll use a **pre-trained embedding model** called [all-MiniLM-L6-v2](https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2) because it:

- **Runs fast** on any computer
- Has a **small size** (~80 MB, not including the size of the standard dependencies required to run an embedding model)
- Provides **good quality** for most use cases
- Allows **local execution** – no internet required after download

## Loading a Model

The following few lines of code are all that's needed to load the model and use it to convert some `text` into vectors:

```py
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# Load the model (downloads automatically the first time)
model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

print(f"Model loaded: {model}")
print(f"Max sequence length: {model.max_seq_length}")

model.encode(text)
```

> [!warning]
> The first time you run this, it downloads the model files. Subsequent runs will load from your local cache.

## Assignment

**Set up and test a pre-trained embedding model** for our movie search system.

1. [ ] Add the `sentence-transformers` package and its sub-dependencies to the project.
   ```sh
   uv add sentence-transformers
   ```
2. [ ] Create a new library module, e.g. `cli/lib/semantic_search.py`, and start by defining a class called `SemanticSearch`. Give it a constructor that initializes the `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` model and assigns it to an instance variable called `self.model`. (You'll also need to import `SentenceTransformer` as shown above.)
3. _Outside_ of the class definition, create a new function called `verify_model` that creates an instance of the `SemanticSearch` class and prints the model information:
   1. [ ] `Model loaded: {MODEL}`, where `{MODEL}` is the `.model` string representation.
   2. [ ] `Max sequence length: {MAX_LENGTH}`, where `{MAX_LENGTH}` is the `.max_seq_length` property of the model.
4. [ ] Back in your semantic search CLI script, create a new `verify` command. It should simply call the `verify_model` function to print the model information.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Model Selection
readme:
# Model Selection

Choosing the right embedding model is _crucial_ for semantic search performance. Different models excel in different scenarios, and the choice affects speed, quality, and resource requirements.

## Leaderboards

There is a [leaderboard of models](https://huggingface.co/spaces/mteb/leaderboard) on Hugging Face that you can use to compare them across many dimensions.

> [!tip]
> At the time of writing, "Cohere" and "Mixedbread" are good default options.

## General Purpose Models

- **Use case:** Broad semantic understanding across domains
- **Examples:** `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, `all-mpnet-base-v2`
- **Best for:** Movie search, general document retrieval

## Domain-Specific Models

- **Use case:** Specialized knowledge (medical, legal, scientific)
- **Examples:** `allenai-specter`, `microsoft/BiomedNLP-PubMedBERT`
- **Best for:** Technical documentation, research papers

## Multilingual Models

- **Use case:** Data in multiple languages in the same search system
- **Examples:** `paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2`
- **Best for:** International movie databases

Because OpenAI will give you those sweet, sweet API credits if you do.

A general-purpose model will always outperform domain-specific or multilingual models.

title: Vector Operations
readme:
# Vector Operations

Vectors are just lists of numbers that represent points in space. The interesting part about them is that _semantically similar words have vectors that are close together_. That means that we need some vector math to be able to see _how close_ two vectors are to each other.

![vector explainer](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/Vectors-1920x1080.mp4)

## Vector Addition

Vector addition is useful for **combining/mixing concepts**. For example, "I want a result that's like this and like that."

With vector addition, we just add the corresponding elements of two vectors together.

```text
[0.5, -0.2,  0.8]
+
[0.1,  0.9, -0.3]
=
[0.6,  0.7,  0.5]
```

"The Great Bear" embeds to `[0.5, -0.2, 0.8]` (Animation, Adventure, Family). Then we add "Back Country" which embeds to `[0.1, 0.9, -0.3]` (Horror, Thriller, Survival). Add them together, and we get the last vector, `[0.6, 0.7, 0.5]`, which represents a _mix_ of those genres (Family, Adventure, Horror).

## Vector Subtraction

Vector subtraction is useful for **removing concepts**. For example, "I want a result that's like this but _not_ that."

```text
[0.5, -0.2,  0.8]
-
[0.1,  0.9, -0.3]
=
[0.4, -1.1,  1.1]
```

Again, "The Great Bear" embeds to `[0.5, -0.2, 0.8]` (Animation, Adventure, Family). If we subtract "The Revenant," which embeds to `[0.1, 0.9, -0.3]` (Horror, Thriller, Survival), we get `[0.4, -1.1, 1.1]`, representing _Family Adventure_ but _not_ Horror or Survival.

## Assignment

**Implement the `add_vectors` and `subtract_vectors` functions.**

1. [ ] `add_vectors(vec1, vec2)` takes two vectors (lists of floats) and returns their _sum_ as a new vector.
   1. [ ] If the input vectors do _not_ have the same length, just raise a `ValueError` with a helpful message.
   2. [ ] Iterate over the indices of both input vectors, and add the corresponding elements together.
   3. [ ] Return the resulting list of sums.
2. [ ] `subtract_vectors(vec1, vec2)` takes two vectors (lists of floats) and returns their _difference_ as a new vector.
   1. [ ] Again, if the input vectors do _not_ have the same length, just raise a `ValueError` with a helpful message.
   2. [ ] Iterate over the indices of both input vectors, and subtract the second vector's element from the first.
   3. [ ] Return the resulting list of differences.

title: Dimensions
readme:
# Dimensions

Vectors are just lists of numbers that represent coordinates in space... _cool_. 2D space is easy: _"3 units right, 2 units up"_ is `[3, 2]`. 3D is also pretty easy; we just add a `z` coordinate: _"3 units right, 2 units up, 1 unit forward"_ is `[3, 2, 1]`.

The problem is, two or three dimensions **is not nearly enough** to represent enough meaningful semantic value. Semantic embeddings are usually in **over 300 dimensions**.

> [!isaac]
> Yes, I know there aren't 300 dimensions (that we know of?!) in the physical universe, but in the mathematical world of vectors, we can have as many dimensions as we want. It's just a list of numbers with 300 entries.

**Example:**

```text
"The Great Bear" → [0.2, -0.8, 0.1, 0.7, -0.3, ...]
"Ted"            → [0.1, -0.9, 0.2, 0.6, -0.2, ...]
"Backcountry"    → [0.8, 0.1, -0.5, 0.2, 0.9, ...]
```

These vectors might have **300+ dimensions**, but the key idea is that **distance in this space represents semantic similarity**.

> [!info]
> Visualizing 300 dimensions is basically impossible. Geoffrey Hinton (often considered the "Godfather of AI") suggested that the way to visualize 14-dimensional vectors is to visualize a 3D space and say "Fourteen!" to yourself very loudly... and we have 300 or more dimensions, not just 14.

title: Dot Product Similarity
readme:
# Dot Product Similarity

Let's learn our first method for measuring similarity between embeddings (vectors): the [**dot product**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_product).

The dot product measures how much two vectors "point in the same direction." It's calculated by multiplying corresponding elements and summing the results. Say we want the dot product of these two vectors:

- `[.8, .5, .5]`
- `[.5, .4, .6]`

First we multiply each pair of corresponding elements:

- `.8 × .5 = 0.4`
- `.5 × .4 = 0.2`
- `.5 × .6 = 0.3`

Then we sum those products:

- `0.4 + 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.9`

The final result is `0.9`. The more similar the vectors are, the _higher_ the dot product will be. If they point in opposite directions, the dot product will be negative.

## Assignment

Later we'll use the [NumPy](https://numpy.org/) library to handle vector math for us. But I want you to do it by hand at least once... _for the learnings_!

**Implement the `dot` function.** It takes two vectors (lists of floats) as input and returns their dot product (a single float).

1. [ ] If `vec1` and `vec2` are not the same length, raise a `ValueError` with a helpful message.
2. [ ] Iterate over the indices of the vectors, multiplying each pair of corresponding elements and adding the result to a running total.
3. [ ] Return the total.

title: Cosine Similarity
readme:
# Cosine Similarity

Dot product has a problem: it's affected by vector magnitude. Vectors have both direction _and_ magnitude (size). The direction is the important part for semantic similarity, but the magnitude (length) of the vector can affect the results of vector operations. For example, these two vectors have identical direction, but different magnitudes:

![vector magnitudes](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/kujsBgD-684x548.png)

In the context of semantic search, we usually _don't care_ about the magnitude of the vectors, just their direction. Magnitude represents "confidence" or "strength." So, for example, say we search for "positive Paddington reviews." We probably want both of these in the results set:

- "Paddington is good" (lower magnitude)
- "Paddington is SO AMAZING" (higher magnitude)

**[Cosine similarity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosine_similarity) solves this** by measuring the _angle_ between vectors, ignoring their length. For example, the dot product of these two vectors:

- `[0.6, 0.8]` (magnitude = 1.0)
- `[3.0, 4.0]` (magnitude = 5.0)

is `5.0`.

The vectors point in exactly the **same direction** (same type of movie), but the dot product is heavily influenced by the magnitude. _For our purposes, we want to ignore the magnitude and just focus on the direction._

**Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle** between two vectors, meaning it only cares about their direction.

This value conventionally ranges from `-1.0` to `1.0`:

- **1.0:** Vectors point in exactly the same direction (perfectly similar)
- **0.0:** Vectors are perpendicular (no similarity)
- **-1.0:** Vectors point in opposite directions (perfectly dissimilar)

![cosine similarity](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/o3TGa7G-1017x720.png)

The formula is as follows:

```text
cosine_similarity = dot_product(A, B) / (magnitude(A) × magnitude(B))
```

And it works in two steps:

1. **Calculate similarity:** The dot product measures how much vectors align
2. **Remove length bias:** Dividing by magnitudes removes the effect of vector size

## Assignment

**Complete the `cosine_similarity` function.**

1. [ ] If the input vectors aren't the same length, just raise a `ValueError`.
2. [ ] Calculate the dot product of the vectors using the `dot` function provided for you.
3. [ ] Calculate the magnitude of each vector by using the provided `euclidean_norm` function provided for you.
   > [!tip]
   > If you're curious, `euclidean_norm` just adds the _squares_ of all the numbers in a vector, then takes the _square root_ of that sum. This should be reminiscent of the [Pythagorean theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem).
4. [ ] If either magnitude is `0`, return `0.0`; this guards against dividing by zero later.
5. [ ] Return the `dot_product` divided by the _product_ (multiplication) of the two magnitudes.

title: Why Cosine Similarity?
readme:
# Why Cosine Similarity?

You might wonder: **why cosine similarity specifically?** Why not some other similarity metric? The answer is simple.

**We want to use the same metric that our embedding model was trained on.**

Embedding models learn to create vectors during training. The **training process** determines which similarity metric works best.

> "We fine-tune the model using a contrastive objective. Formally, we compute the **cosine similarity** from each possible sentence pair from the batch."
>
> – [`all-MiniLM-L6-v2` documentation](https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2#fine-tuning)

Because our model was trained using **cosine similarity**, we should also use **cosine similarity** for search.

## Cosine Similarity Is Common

**Good news!** Most sentence embedding models are trained with cosine similarity:

- `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`: cosine similarity
- `all-mpnet-base-v2`: cosine similarity
- Other `sentence-transformers` models usually use cosine similarity

> [!warning]
> When using other embedding models in the future, double-check their documentation to be sure!

title: Generating Text Embeddings
readme:
# Generating Text Embeddings

Let's learn to convert some of our text into embeddings!

## Preprocessing

Unlike keyword search, embeddings handle many preprocessing tasks automatically:

- **Case insensitive:** "PADDINGTON" and "paddington" get similar embeddings
- **Punctuation robust:** "Ted!" and "Ted" are nearly identical
- **Context aware:** "bear" in "teddy bear" vs. "grizzly bear" gets different vectors

## Assignment

**Add a function to the semantic search module that generates an embedding for a single text input, and verify that it works.**

1. [ ] In `cli/lib/semantic_search.py` (or whatever you named your semantic search library module), add a `generate_embedding(self, text)` method to the `SemanticSearch` class:
   1. [ ] If the input `text` is empty or contains only whitespace, raise a `ValueError` with a helpful message.
   2. [ ] Use the [`encode`](https://sbert.net/docs/package_reference/sentence_transformer/SentenceTransformer.html#sentence_transformers.SentenceTransformer.encode) method of the model to generate an `embedding` for the input text. The `encode` method expects a _list_ of inputs, but we're only passing in one, so be sure to wrap it in a list. It also returns a corresponding list as output, but we only care about the first element because we're only passing in one input.
   3. [ ] Return the `embedding`, using the first index of the result from `encode`.
2. [ ] Add a new top-level function to the same module: `embed_text(text)`.
   1. [ ] Create an instance of the `SemanticSearch` class.
   2. [ ] Get the embedding for the input text.
   3. [ ] Print the following:
      ```py
      print(f"Text: {text}")
      print(f"First 3 dimensions: {embedding[:3]}")
      print(f"Dimensions: {embedding.shape[0]}")
      ```
3. [ ] In your CLI script, add a new `embed_text` command that accepts a single string argument. It should run your `embed_text` function with the provided text.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

> [!info]
> An embedding model is trained with a unique [tokenizer](https://huggingface.co/learn/llm-course/chapter2/4). You must tokenize in the same way that the model was trained, so models actually come packaged with their tokenizers... In other words, **it's done for you by the library**!

title: Document Embeddings
readme:
# Document Embeddings

Now that we've created an embedding for a single string, let's create embeddings for our entire movie dataset. This will transform our movies into a searchable vector database.

We'll save it in a [`.npy` file](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.lib.format.html) by using [`numpy.save`](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.save.html#numpy.save). The `.npy` file will be our **vector store**.

## Assignment

**Add logic to generate embeddings for the entire movie dataset; to save them to disk; to load them from disk if they already exist; and to verify that the embeddings seem correct.** This way, the time-consuming embedding generation step only needs to be done once.

1. [ ] Add `numpy` as a dependency.
   ```sh
   uv add numpy==2.3.3
   ```
2. [ ] In `cli/lib/semantic_search.py` (or whatever you named your semantic search library module), add the import at the top of the file:
   ```py
   import numpy as np
   ```
3. [ ] In the `SemanticSearch` class, initialize a few more values:
   1. [ ] `self.embeddings` to `None`
   2. [ ] `self.documents` to `None`
   3. [ ] `self.document_map` to an empty dictionary
4. [ ] Add a new `build_embeddings(self, documents)` method. `documents` is a list of dictionaries, each representing a movie.
   1. [ ] Set `self.documents` equal to the `documents` argument.
   2. [ ] For each document, add an entry to `self.document_map` where the key is the `id` of the document and the value is the document itself.
   3. [ ] Create a string representation of each document (movie) and store them all in a list. Each string should have this format: `f"{doc['title']}: {doc['description']}"`
   4. [ ] Use the model's `encode` method on the list of movie strings. Set the `show_progress_bar` argument on `encode` to `True` so you can see the progress (it takes a while). Store the result as `self.embeddings`.
   5. [ ] Save the embeddings into `cache/movie_embeddings.npy` using [`np.save`](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.save.html#numpy.save).
   6. [ ] Return `self.embeddings` from the method.
5. [ ] We want to build the embeddings only once, and thereafter load them from disk. Add a new `load_or_create_embeddings(self, documents)` method to the `SemanticSearch` class.
   1. [ ] Populate `self.documents` and `self.document_map` in this method as well, just like in `build_embeddings`.
   2. [ ] Check if the `cache/movie_embeddings.npy` file [`exists`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.exists).
      1. [ ] If it does, call [`np.load`](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/generated/numpy.load.html#numpy.load) to load the embeddings from that file into `self.embeddings`.
      2. [ ] Verify that the length of `self.embeddings` is equal to the length of `documents`. _If it is_, return the cached `self.embeddings`.
   3. [ ] Otherwise, return the result of rebuilding the embeddings from scratch with `self.build_embeddings(documents)`.
6. [ ] Add a new top-level `verify_embeddings` function. It should:
   1. [ ] Create an instance of `SemanticSearch`.
   2. [ ] Load the documents from `movies.json` into a list.
   3. [ ] Call `load_or_create_embeddings` with those movie documents.
   4. [ ] Print the number of documents and the shape of the embeddings:
      ```py
      print(f"Number of docs:   {len(documents)}")
      print(f"Embeddings shape: {embeddings.shape[0]} vectors in {embeddings.shape[1]} dimensions")
      ```
7. [ ] Add a `verify_embeddings` command to your semantic search CLI script. It should simply call the `verify_embeddings` function.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Query Embeddings
readme:
# Query Embeddings

We need to convert user queries into vectors, just like we did with our documents. That way we can actually compare the query to them. For example, say we have these vectors in our document vector store:

- `[1,2,3]`
- `[4,5,6]`
- `[7,8,9]`

Which one is closest to the query "Bear"?

... It's impossible to say without first converting "Bear" into a similar type of vector. So, `"bear" –> [2, 3, 4]` (for example).

Now, what's closest to `[2, 3, 4]`? _We can use vector math to find out!_

## Preprocessing for Embeddings

Remember that embedding models handle many preprocessing tasks automatically:

- **Case insensitive:** "FUNNY" and "funny" get similar embeddings
- **Punctuation robust:** "movies!" and "movies" are nearly identical
- **Whitespace tolerant:** Extra spaces are handled gracefully

This means we can use minimal preprocessing for queries – just basic cleaning like stripping whitespace.

## Assignment

**Implement query embedding functionality that converts user search queries into embedding vectors.**

1. [ ] Add a new `embed_query_text(query)` function to your library module:
   1. [ ] Create an instance of the `SemanticSearch` class.
   2. [ ] Call your existing `generate_embedding` method with the provided query (this method already handles whitespace stripping and validation).
   3. [ ] Print information about the query and its embedding:
      ```py
      print(f"Query: {query}")
      print(f"First 3 dimensions: {embedding[:3]}")
      print(f"Shape: {embedding.shape}")
      ```
2. [ ] In your CLI script, add a new `embed_query` command that accepts a positional query string argument. It should call your `embed_query_text` function with the provided query.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

CLICommand.Command: uv run cli/semantic_search_cli.py embed_query "funny bear movies"
uv run cli/semantic_search_cli.py embed_query "FUNNY bear   MOVIES"

title: Same Model
readme:
# Same Model

**You must use the same embedding model** for both documents and queries. Different models create _incompatible vector spaces_. Everything in magical vector land is _relative_. The word "king" might embed to `[0.5, 0.2, 0.1]` in one model and `[0.6, 0.3, 0.2]` in another.

```py
# ✅ Correct – same model
model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
doc_embeddings = model.encode(documents)
query_embeddings = model.encode(queries)

# ❌ Wrong – different models create incompatible spaces
doc_model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
query_model = SentenceTransformer('all-mpnet-base-v2')
```

Why? Each model learns a different mathematical space. It's like trying to compare GPS coordinates with street addresses – they're incompatible.

title: Implementing Semantic Search
readme:
# Implementing Semantic Search

Now we'll combine everything we've learned to build a complete semantic search engine! This will find movies based on _meaning_ rather than exact keyword matches. Our semantic search system has five main steps:

1. **Embed documents** (done once) – Convert movies to vectors
2. **Store documents** (done once) – Store embedded documents in vector store
3. **Embed query** (per search) – Convert user query to vector
4. **Calculate similarities** – Compare query vector to all movie vectors
5. **Rank and return** – Sort by similarity and return top results

## Cosine Similarity

For semantic search, we use cosine similarity to compare vectors. It measures the angle between two vectors, giving us a score from `-1.0` to `1.0`:

- **1.0:** Vectors point in the same direction (identical meaning)
- **0.0:** Vectors are perpendicular (unrelated)
- **-1.0:** Vectors point in opposite directions (opposite meaning)

## Assignment

**Build a complete semantic search system** that finds movies by meaning. You'll combine all the pieces you've built in previous lessons.

1. [ ] Add a `cosine_similarity(vec1, vec2)` function to the module (outside the class). You implemented this algorithm from scratch earlier in the chapter, but in practice you will typically use a version that's optimized and written in a fast language like C. NumPy is a convenient library that offers many vector operations in Python, which are actually quite fast because under the hood the operations are done in C. Copy and paste the following code into your module:

   ```py
   def cosine_similarity(vec1, vec2):
       dot_product = np.dot(vec1, vec2)
       norm1 = np.linalg.norm(vec1)
       norm2 = np.linalg.norm(vec2)

       if norm1 == 0 or norm2 == 0:
           return 0.0

       return dot_product / (norm1 * norm2)
   ```

2. [ ] Add a `search(self, query, limit)` method to the `SemanticSearch` class:
   1. [ ] Check if embeddings are loaded. If not, raise a `ValueError` with this message:
      ```text
      "No embeddings loaded. Call `load_or_create_embeddings` first."
      ```
   2. [ ] Generate an embedding for the query using `self.generate_embedding(query)`.
   3. [ ] Calculate cosine similarity between the query embedding and each document embedding.
   4. [ ] Create a list of (similarity_score, document) tuples.
   5. [ ] Sort the list by similarity score in descending order.
   6. [ ] Return the top results (up to `limit`) as a list of dictionaries, each containing:
      - `score`: The cosine similarity score
      - `title`: The movie title
      - `description`: The movie description
3. [ ] In your CLI script, add a new `search` command:
   1. [ ] It should accept a positional query string argument.
   2. [ ] Accept an optional `--limit` argument (default 5).
   3. [ ] It should create a `SemanticSearch` instance.
   4. [ ] Load movies and load/create embeddings.
   5. [ ] Call the `search` method with the query and limit.
   6. [ ] Print the results in this format:

      ```text
      1. Spaceflight IC-1: An Adventure in Space (score: 0.4406)
        The opening narrative is given by a man in a high ranking military uniform. He tells us the film is ...

      2. Adventureland (score: 0.4150)
        In 1987, James Brennan (Jesse Eisenberg) has two plans. The first plan is to have a summer vacation ...

      3. Odyssey 5 (score: 0.4038)
        The story follows six people on a routine flight of the space shuttle Odyssey, on August 7, 2007: fo...
      ```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.
